target.title: CLI: prefer bundled installs for scoped plugin specs
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw plugins install @openclaw/feishu` can create a duplicate global plugin copy even when the same plugin is already bundled.
- Why it matters: the install command reports success, but runtime discovery/load still prefers the bundled plugin, so the newly installed global copy does not actually take effect.
- What changed: scoped npm specs now participate in the bundled pre-install resolution path, so installs like `@openclaw/feishu` resolve to the bundled plugin by default instead of creating a duplicate global install.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change runtime duplicate resolution or plugin origin precedence during discovery/load.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw plugins install @openclaw/<plugin>` now prefers a matching bundled plugin before attempting npm installation.

If a bundled plugin matches the requested npm spec, OpenClaw enables the bundled plugin instead of creating a duplicate global plugin install.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows
- Runtime/container: local dev checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): bundled plugin install flow
- Relevant config (redacted): default local config

### Steps

1. Use a build that bundles `@openclaw/feishu`.
2. Run `openclaw plugins install @openclaw/feishu`.
3. Observe whether OpenClaw creates a duplicate global plugin copy or resolves to bundled.

### Expected

- A matching bundled plugin should be selected by default.
- No duplicate global plugin copy should be created for the same plugin id.

### Actual

- Before this change, scoped npm specs bypassed the bundled pre-install path.
- This could create a duplicate global install that runtime later marked as overridden by the bundled plugin.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm test src/cli/plugin-install-plan.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/plugins/bundled-sources.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/commands/onboarding/plugin-install.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/plugins/loader.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`
- Edge cases checked:
  - bare plugin-id specs still resolve to bundled
  - scoped npm specs now resolve to bundled when matched
  - unmatched scoped npm specs still return no bundled pre-plan
  - existing onboarding and loader behavior still passes unchanged
- What I did **not** verify:
  - full `pnpm check` is currently blocked by unrelated existing `extensions/tlon/**` type-resolution errors for `@tloncorp/api`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR
- Files/config to restore: `src/cli/plugin-install-plan.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - scoped bundled plugin installs unexpectedly falling through to npm install again

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk:
  - A scoped npm package could now resolve to bundled earlier than before.
- Mitigation:
  - The change is limited to explicit bundled matches and is covered by targeted tests.

## AI Assistance

This PR was AI-assisted. I reviewed the code changes and ran the verification commands listed above.

target.title: feat: cute GTK-native Linux App (#75)
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: - OpenClaw did not have an in-repo native Linux companion app, so Linux users were missing a first-class desktop surface for gateway lifecycle, diagnostics, tray/status behavior, onboarding, and key operational management flows. - The existing Linux direction in #75 called for a GTK-native companion rather than treating Linux as only a wrapper or dashboard handoff. - Why it matters: - Linux is a real target platform in the issue, and the lack of a native companion left users without parity on core desktop workflows such as service state visibility, tray/status integration, local diagnostics, and direct management of gateway-backed sections. - Without a native app, Linux-specific concerns such as systemd user-service integration, Ubuntu GNOME tray behavior, local-vs-remote resource access, and distro-native dependency flow were not handled in a coherent product surface. - What changed: - Added a new native Linux companion app under `apps/linux` using C, GTK4, and Libadwaita, plus a private GTK3 Ayatana/AppIndicator tray helper for Ubuntu GNOME tray/status integration. - Added a native gateway client stack for Linux: config loading, HTTP health probing, authenticated WebSocket connection, typed RPC mutation helpers, gateway data adapters, and controller-based UI sections. - Added native operational management surfaces for Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances, including config-safe editing, locality-aware transcript/log opening, pairing actions, and richer management flows previously absent on Linux. - Added Linux runtime/readiness modeling, onboarding, diagnostics, notifications, systemd integration, and focused automated tests covering parser behavior, mutation request shapes, RPC lifecycle handling, config rebuild safety, runtime/readiness logic, and helper behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): - This PR does not add distro packaging artifacts such as `.deb`, `.rpm`, or Snap packages. - This PR does not attempt to solve the Windows side. - This PR does not claim full macOS parity across every desktop capability; it establishes the native Linux companion foundation and a substantial in-app management surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #75 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: - N/A - Missing detection / guardrail: - N/A - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - N/A - Why this regressed now: - N/A - If unknown, what was ruled out: - N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - N/A - Scenario the test should lock in: - N/A - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - N/A - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - N/A - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Linux now has a native GTK4 + Libadwaita companion app in-repo. - Ubuntu GNOME users get tray/status integration through the companion plus the private AppIndicator helper. - The Linux app can discover and work with the user `openclaw-gateway.service` lifecycle through native systemd integration. - The app surfaces first-run progression clearly across the main readiness states: - before `openclaw setup` - after `openclaw setup` but before gateway install - after gateway install with the service active and the gateway answering - The app surfaces runtime/readiness/onboarding/diagnostics states directly in the Linux UI. - Channels can now be inspected and managed in-app, including probe, logout, config editing, and WhatsApp QR linking flow. - Skills can now be filtered and managed in-app, including enable/disable, install, and env/API-key updates. - Sessions can now be managed in-app, including patch/reset/compact/delete and log access. - Cron can now be managed in-app, including scheduler status, recent runs, create/edit, run-now, enable/disable, delete, and transcript access. - Instances now expose richer remote node details, pending pairing approval/reject flows, and copy-debug-info behavior. - Log/transcript opening is locality-aware: local resources open locally when available, otherwise the UI falls back safely. - Channel config editing now validates edited JSON, preserves unrelated config keys, and saves using `baseHash`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user on Linux] -> [CLI / dashboard / partial prototype paths] -> [limited lifecycle visibility and little or no native management] After: [user on Linux] -> [native GTK4 companion app] -> [systemd + HTTP + authenticated WebSocket runtime model] -> [tray / diagnostics / onboarding / management sections] -> [native actions for channels, skills, sessions, cron, instances] ```` ## Security Impact (required) * New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: * The Linux companion introduces a new native desktop surface that can read local runtime context, inspect service state, connect to the local gateway over HTTP/WebSocket, edit config, open local logs/transcripts when available, and trigger gateway-backed management mutations. * Risk is mitigated by keeping the runtime model explicit, separating systemd discovery from gateway RPC transport, validating config JSON before save, preserving unrelated config content during rebuild, using `baseHash` on config writes, hardening request shapes through typed mutation helpers, and covering lifecycle/parsing/request-shape behavior in targeted tests. * Local-vs-remote resource access is handled explicitly so the app does not blindly assume every gateway-returned path is locally openable. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment * OS: * Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME * Runtime/container: * Bare-metal / local user-session workflow with systemd user service * Model/provider: * Local gateway-backed companion flow; model/provider depends on gateway config * Integration/channel (if any): * systemd user service, HTTP health, authenticated gateway WebSocket, GTK4 + Libadwaita, Ayatana/AppIndicator * Relevant config (redacted): * Fresh local OpenClaw config and user `openclaw-gateway.service`; secrets redacted ### Steps 1. Install Linux build dependencies and clone/build OpenClaw. 2. Build and run the Linux app with Meson. 3. Launch the Linux companion app before running `openclaw setup`. 4. Run `openclaw setup`, then relaunch or refresh the companion app. 5. Install and start the user gateway service. 6. Launch or refresh the Linux companion app again. 7. Exercise tray/status, diagnostics, onboarding/readiness, and the Dashboard / General / Channels / Skills / Sessions / Cron / Instances sections. 8. Run the Linux test suite. ### Expected * Before setup, the app reports the missing setup/runtime state clearly. * After setup but before gateway install, the app reports configured-but-not-installed service state clearly. * After gateway install/start, the app reports a healthy connected state, with diagnostics, dashboard, and general surfaces reflecting the running gateway. * The Linux app builds, launches, connects through the native runtime model, reflects systemd/gateway state accurately, and exposes native management flows for the major gateway-backed sections. * The Linux test suite passes. ### Actual * Matches expected in local verification. ## Evidence Attach at least one: * [x] Failing test/log before + passing after * [x] Trace/log snippets * [x] Screenshot/recording * [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What I personally verified (not just CI), and how: * Verified scenarios: * Built the Linux app from a clean checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME. * Ran the Meson test suite locally. * Verified tray helper spawn and tray/status behavior. * Verified systemd user-service discovery and lifecycle reflection. * Verified diagnostics/readiness/runtime presentation. * Verified first-run progression manually with screenshots: * app launched before `openclaw setup` * app after `openclaw setup` * app after gateway install/start with the gateway answering properly * Verified the healthy connected state manually against a fresh configured local gateway. * Verified additional Linux app surfaces manually, including Dashboard, General, and Diagnostics. * Verified the main Linux companion window and controller-backed sections load and refresh. * Verified native management flows across Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances at the implementation level. * Verified config editing flow behavior, including full-document rebuild and hash-aware save path. * Verified locality-aware log/transcript fallback behavior. * Edge cases checked: * Invalid config JSON aborts save. * Config rebuild preserves unrelated top-level keys and sibling channel config. * Mutation request shapes match the intended backend contract structure. * RPC lifecycle handling covers success/error/timeout/disconnect cleanup paths. * Tray-helper lifecycle and cleanup behavior were tightened. * Readiness states remain intelligible across the bootstrap sequence from no setup -> configured -> installed/running. * What I did **not** verify: * Official distro packaging artifacts (`.deb`, `.rpm`, Snap) are not included here. * Broad desktop-environment compatibility beyond the Ubuntu GNOME target path. * Full end-to-end parity for every macOS companion capability. ## Review Conversations * [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. * [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration * Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) * No * Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) * No * If yes, exact upgrade steps: * N/A ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. * Risk: * Linux desktop behavior can vary by distro and shell, especially around tray hosting. * Mitigation: * Target the initial implementation clearly at Ubuntu GNOME, use a private AppIndicator helper, and keep the helper boundary isolated from the GTK4 main process. * Risk: * The Linux companion now depends on a larger native runtime stack: systemd context, HTTP health, WebSocket RPC, and gateway payload parsing. * Mitigation: * Keep those concerns separated into dedicated modules and cover parser/RPC/mutation behavior with focused tests. * Risk: * Config editing could accidentally overwrite unrelated config content if the save path is too narrow. * Mitigation: * Load the full config document, edit only the relevant subtree, rebuild the full document, validate JSON before save, and send `baseHash` with `config.set`. * Risk: * Transcript/log opening can fail or behave incorrectly if the gateway resource is not local to the GTK host. * Mitigation: * Enforce locality-aware fallback logic and only open local resources directly when they are actually present and accessible. * Risk: * This is a large first-class app addition and therefore a large review surface. * Mitigation: * Keep the app organized into focused subsystems and section controllers, provide a documented Linux build/test path, and include targeted tests for the most contract-sensitive areas.

target.title: fix(secrets): audit plaintext config backups
target.body:
## Summary
- scan adjacent OpenClaw config backup files during `openclaw secrets audit`
- report plaintext secrets left in backup config JSON with the backup file path
- add regression coverage for a `.bak` config containing a provider API key

/claim #11829

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw secrets audit` now scans adjacent config backup files such as `openclaw.json.bak` and reports plaintext provider API keys found there.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, running the real `pnpm --silent openclaw secrets audit --json` CLI against a temporary config directory outside the unit-test harness.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created a temporary OpenClaw config directory containing `openclaw.json` plus an adjacent `openclaw.json.bak` with a test provider key, then ran:

```console
HOME=/tmp/openclaw-proof-clean-xTBrff/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-proof-clean-xTBrff/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-proof-clean-xTBrff/openclaw.json pnpm --silent openclaw secrets audit --json
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real CLI run:

```json
{
  "status": "findings",
  "filesScanned": [
    "/tmp/openclaw-proof-clean-xTBrff/openclaw.json",
    "/tmp/openclaw-proof-clean-xTBrff/openclaw.json.bak"
  ],
  "summary": {
    "plaintextCount": 1,
    "unresolvedRefCount": 0,
    "shadowedRefCount": 0,
    "legacyResidueCount": 0
  },
  "findings": [
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "file": "/tmp/openclaw-proof-clean-xTBrff/openclaw.json.bak",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext."
    }
  ]
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The audit includes both `openclaw.json` and `openclaw.json.bak` in `filesScanned`, and reports `PLAINTEXT_FOUND` at `models.providers.openai.apiKey` for the backup file.

**What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

## Verification
- `pnpm test src/secrets/audit.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/storage-scan.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/storage-scan.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts`

Note: `pnpm tsgo:core:test` currently fails in unrelated browser-import tests because `esbuild` cannot be resolved in this local clone.

target.title: docs(tools): document owner-only tools and commands.ownerAllowFrom
target.body:
## Summary

Add documentation for owner-only tools and `commands.ownerAllowFrom` configuration, addressing #53058.

### Problem

Several tools (`gateway`, `cron`, `nodes`) are gated behind `ownerOnly: true` in source but this isn't documented anywhere. Users with `tools.profile: "full"` are confused when agents can't access these tools and silently fall back to CLI workarounds. The only way to discover this was reading source code.

### Changes

Added an "Owner-only tools" section to `docs/tools/index.md` covering:

- **Which tools require ownership** — `gateway`, `cron`, `nodes` listed in a table
- **How to configure** — `commands.ownerAllowFrom` with channel-native sender IDs
- **Examples** — Discord, WhatsApp, Telegram ID formats
- **How to find your sender ID** — gateway logs and Discord Developer Mode
- **Troubleshooting** — explains the silent fallback behavior

### Source references

- `src/agents/tool-policy.ts` — owner filtering logic
- `src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.ts`, `cron-tool.ts`, `nodes-tool.ts` — `ownerOnly: true`
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` — existing but undocumented help string

Closes #53058

target.title: fix(agents): restore correct subagent model precedence in resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult
target.body:
## Summary

PR #58003 (commit e394262bd8) introduced a regression in `agent-scope.ts`: `agentConfig.model` (the agent's own model) was placed before `cfg.agents.defaults.subagents.model` (the global subagent default) in the `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` candidates array. This shadowed the global default for any agent that has its own primary model configured — the common case.

#81783 fixed the same regression in `model-selection.ts` but the `agent-scope.ts` path was missed. This path is used by the cron isolation runner (`src/cron/isolated-agent/model-selection.ts`) and `resolveSubagentModelFallbacksOverride`.

Correct precedence (matching `model-selection.ts:329–331`):

1. `agentConfig.subagents.model` — per-agent subagent config (highest priority)
2. `cfg.agents.defaults.subagents.model` — global subagent default
3. `agentConfig.model` — agent's own model (last resort only)

Closes #58822.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: agents with their own primary model but no per-agent `subagents.model` have subagents spawned with the agent's own model instead of the global subagent default.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw clone on branch `fix/agents-subagent-model-precedence-agent-scope` (2026-05-20).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
npx vitest run src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-fallback-policy.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

Evidence after fix:
```
 ✓ |agents-core| agent-scope > resolveAgentConfig > resolves the subagent model config selected for isolated runs 0ms
 ✓ |agents-core| agent-scope > resolveAgentConfig > resolves subagent model fallbacks from the selected subagent model source 0ms
 ✓ |agents-core| agent-scope > resolveAgentConfig > uses subagent model fallbacks for auto-selected spawned subagent models 0ms
 ✓ |agents-support| agent-scope > resolveAgentConfig > resolves the subagent model config selected for isolated runs 0ms
 ✓ |cron| model formatting and precedence edge cases > config model format variations > prefers agents.defaults.subagents.model over the agent's own model 0ms
 ✓ |cron| run-fallback-policy > resolveCronFallbacksOverride > uses global subagent model fallbacks when agent has no per-agent subagents.model 0ms
 ✓ |cron| run-fallback-policy > resolveCronFallbacksOverride > uses subagent model fallbacks when cron selects the configured subagent model 0ms

 Test Files  4 passed (4)
      Tests  127 passed (127)
   Duration  6.84s (transform 5.33s, setup 920ms, import 5.10s, tests 277ms, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` returns `default-subagent` source (not `agent` source) when an agent has its own model but no per-agent subagents override.

What was not tested: live multi-agent runs with mixed model configs.

target.title: fix: resolve Windows .cmd shims to underlying .exe before spawn
target.body:
## Summary

  - Problem: On Windows, every `provider=claude-cli` spawn fails at the supervisor with `spawn claude ENOENT`; the
   fallback chain cascades and the user sees `Embedded agent failed before reply`.
  - Why it matters: Claude CLI is unusable on Windows — Discord, webchat, and any other channel routing to
  `claude-cli` is broken.
  - What changed: `.cmd` shims are now resolved to their underlying `.exe` (or `node.exe <cli.js>`) and spawned
  directly, bypassing Node's CVE-2024-27980 restriction on `.cmd` and `cmd.exe`'s argument-escaping limits.
  `"claude"` added to the supervisor's `cmdCommands` allowlist.
  - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no non-Windows behavior changed, `resolveChildProcessInvocation` in
  `exec.ts` is untouched, and shims with non-npm-bin bodies fall through to the original spawn path.

  ## Change Type (select all)

  - [x] Bug fix
  - [ ] Feature
  - [ ] Refactor required for the fix
  - [ ] Docs
  - [ ] Security hardening
  - [ ] Chore/infra

  ## Scope (select all touched areas)

  - [x] Gateway / orchestration
  - [ ] Skills / tool execution
  - [ ] Auth / tokens
  - [ ] Memory / storage
  - [ ] Integrations
  - [ ] API / contracts
  - [ ] UI / DX
  - [ ] CI/CD / infra

  ## Linked Issue/PR

  - Closes #68788
  - Related N/A
  - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

  ## Root Cause (if applicable)

  - Root cause: `"claude"` was missing from the supervisor's `cmdCommands` allowlist, so
  `resolveWindowsCommandShim` returned `"claude"` unchanged and `spawn("claude", …)` failed ENOENT. Adding
  `"claude"` then surfaces a second bug: Node >=18.20.2 rejects `spawn("claude.cmd", …)` with EINVAL
  (CVE-2024-27980), and wrapping via `cmd.exe /d /s /c` fails because system prompts contain `\r \n & | < > ^ %`
  which `cmd.exe` cannot parse.
  - Missing detection / guardrail: no coverage for spawning non-`npm` `.cmd` shims through the supervisor's child
  adapter on Windows.
  - Contributing context (if known): `resolveNpmArgvForWindows` in `src/process/exec.ts` already solves the
  `.cmd`-vs-CVE problem for `npm`/`npx`, but the supervisor's `createChildAdapter` does not route through
  `resolveChildProcessInvocation`, so it did not inherit that protection.

  ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

  - Coverage level that should have caught this:
    - [x] Unit test
    - [ ] Seam / integration test
    - [ ] End-to-end test
    - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
  - Target test or file: `src/process/windows-command.test.ts`
  - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveWindowsCmdShimArgv` rewrites `argv[0]` from a `.cmd` shim to its
  underlying `.exe`, prepends `process.execPath` for `.js` targets, performs PATH lookup for bare names, and
  leaves `argv` unchanged on non-Windows / missing file / non-matching shim body.
  - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely in how the supervisor resolves the command
   before spawn, so unit-testing the resolver covers it without a full Windows spawn harness.
  - Existing test that already covers this (if any): none
  - If no new test is added, why not: N/A

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes

  - `claude-cli`-backed providers now spawn successfully on Windows where they previously failed with `spawn
  claude ENOENT`.

  Security Impact (required)

  - New permissions/capabilities? (No)
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
  - New/changed network calls? (No)
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes but narrowly)
  - Data access scope changed? (No)
  - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: The helper reads argv[0] as a path and spawns the .exe it names. The
  path source is unchanged — it comes from OpenClaw's own resolveCommand output and process.env.PATH, same as
  before. The regex matches only the narrow npm-bin shim pattern ("%dp0%…target.(exe|js)" %*); any non-matching
  shim falls through and the original argv is spawned as-is. No new untrusted input is introduced.

  Repro + Verification

  Environment

  - OS: Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200 (x64)
  - Runtime/container: Node.js v22.22.2
  - Model/provider: claude-cli (Anthropic @anthropic-ai/claude-code, claude.cmd on PATH)
  - Integration/channel (if any): Discord (also reproduces on webchat)
  - Relevant config (redacted): default, Claude CLI authenticated via claude login

  Steps

  1. npm i -g openclaw on Windows.
  2. npm i -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code.
  3. openclaw gateway start, send a message via Discord.

  Expected

  - Message routes to claude-cli, the CLI spawns, bot replies.

  Actual

  [process/supervisor] spawn failed: reason=Error: spawn claude ENOENT
  [model-fallback/decision] decision=candidate_failed ... detail=spawn claude ENOENT
  (repeats across opus-4-7, sonnet-4-6, opus-4-6, opus-4-5, sonnet-4-5, haiku-4-5)
  Embedded agent failed before reply: spawn claude ENOENT

  Evidence

  - Failing test/log before + passing after
  - Trace/log snippets
  - Screenshot/recording
  - Perf numbers (if relevant)

  After pnpm test src/process/windows-command.test.ts: 10 passed. After live Discord run: claude.exe spawns with
  an absolute path, bot replies normally, no supervisor errors.

  Human Verification (required)

  - Verified scenarios: end-to-end Discord message flow on Windows 11 (fails before, succeeds after); pnpm check;
  pnpm build; bundled dist wires the new helper through correctly.
  - Edge cases checked: bare-name .cmd resolved via PATH; .js target resolved to node.exe <cli.js>; missing shim
  file and non-matching shim body both fall through unchanged.
  - What I did not verify: .bat shim bodies (only .cmd tested); non-npm shim formats (chocolatey/scoop) — these
  fall through unchanged but aren't exercised.

  Review Conversations

  - I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
  - I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

  Compatibility / Migration

  - Backward compatible? (Yes)
  - Config/env changes? (No)
  - Migration needed? (No)

  Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: a hand-written .cmd whose body coincidentally matches the regex but also does additional batch work (env
   sets, chained commands) would be bypassed in favor of the detected target.
    - Mitigation: the regex is anchored to the quoted-target + %* shape npm/pnpm/yarn shims use; custom batch
  scripts with control flow won't match and fall through untouched.

target.title: Improve managed child-task flow UX for long-running tasks
target.body:
## Summary

This PR improves the long-running Task Flow path for managed child-task spawns created via `sessions_spawn(taskFlow=...)`.

It turns the flow from a mostly internal state machine into a more user-facing and durable experience:

- `sessions_spawn(taskFlow=...)` can create managed flows for child-task work
- child task completion now automatically closes the parent managed flow
- managed child-task flows now surface minimal user-visible progress notifications
- noisy nonterminal progress updates are throttled/aggregated
- blocked vs retryable failures now expose clearer next-action guidance
- retryable managed child-task flows now have a real retry command/operation, not just hints

## What changed

### 1. Managed flow creation from `sessions_spawn`
- add `taskFlow` support to `sessions_spawn`
- link spawned child tasks to `parentFlowId`
- mark the managed flow as waiting on the child task
- persist enough launch metadata under `stateJson.launch` for later retries

### 2. Auto-close managed flows on child completion
- close managed flows when the tracked child task reaches a terminal state
- propagate completion context into flow `stateJson`
- preserve special cases like `cancelled`, `lost`, and `blocked`

### 3. Minimal user-facing progress delivery
- add start / waiting / terminal notifications for managed child-task flows
- prefer direct requester delivery when possible, with existing fallback behavior otherwise
- use idempotent per-flow revision delivery keys

### 4. Throttling + guidance
- debounce noisy nonterminal `queued/running/waiting` chatter for managed child-task flows when `notifyPolicy: "state_changes"`
- keep first waiting notification and terminal/blocked updates immediate
- expose lightweight resolution hints:
  - `blocked` => `user_action_required`
  - `failed` / `lost` => `retry_available`
- surface `resolution` / `nextAction` in runtime + CLI flow views

### 5. Real retry operation loop
- add real retry entrypoints for eligible managed child-task flows
- restrict retry to sane terminal states (`failed`, `lost`, `blocked`)
- re-launch the child task and move the flow back into `waiting` / `wait_worker`
- clear stale completion/error state and rewrite `waitJson`
- expose retry in runtime (`runtime.taskFlow.retryChildTask(...)`)
- expose retry in CLI:
  - `openclaw tasks flow retry <lookup>`
  - `flows show` prints `retryCommand: openclaw tasks flow retry <id>` when retryable

## Notes

I kept the scope intentionally tight and backward-compatible:

- no large new delivery subsystem
- throttling only applies to managed child-task progress chatter
- blocked child completion now stays `blocked` rather than collapsing into generic `failed`
- retry depends on persisted launch metadata, so older flows may not always be reconstructable
- attachment-dependent child spawns are not yet fully reconstructable for retry

## Validation

Passed targeted tests across:

- `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`
- `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-taskflow.test.ts`
- `src/tasks/task-flow-registry.test.ts`
- `src/tasks/task-executor.test.ts`
- `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-tasks.test.ts`
- `src/commands/flows.test.ts`
- `src/cli/program/register.status-health-sessions.test.ts`

Representative commands used:

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts \
  src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts \
  src/plugins/runtime/runtime-taskflow.test.ts \
  src/tasks/task-flow-registry.test.ts \
  src/tasks/task-executor.test.ts \
  src/plugins/runtime/runtime-tasks.test.ts

pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts \
  src/tasks/task-executor.test.ts \
  src/plugins/runtime/runtime-taskflow.test.ts \
  src/commands/flows.test.ts

pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts \
  src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts \
  src/cli/program/register.status-health-sessions.test.ts
```

I also ran targeted TypeScript checks while working through the series.

## Caveat

Repo-wide `tsgo` / `tsc` still surfaces a pre-existing unrelated issue in `src/gateway/server.config-apply.test.ts`. That issue is outside this Task Flow patch series.

target.title: fix(agents): thread errorMessage through finalizeTransportStream
target.body:
## Summary

`finalizeTransportStream` throws when a stream stops with `stopReason === "error"` or `"aborted"`. The thrown error was hardcoded to `"An unknown error occurred"`, silently discarding `output.errorMessage` — which `failTransportStream` always populates before marking the stream as failed. Every provider transport failure surfaced the same opaque string regardless of the actual cause.

The fix reads `output.errorMessage` at throw-time with a null-coalesce fallback, preserving the `"An unknown error occurred"` string for any path that bypasses `failTransportStream`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `finalizeTransportStream` throws `"An unknown error occurred"` regardless of the actual error, discarding `output.errorMessage` set at `transport-stream-shared.ts:133`.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw clone on branch `fix/agents-transport-stream-error-message` (2026-05-20).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
npx vitest run src/agents/transport-stream-shared.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

Evidence after fix:
```
 ✓ |agents-core| transport stream shared helpers > throws with the original error message when stream failed 1ms
 ✓ |agents-core| transport stream shared helpers > throws with the abort error message when stream was aborted 0ms
 ✓ |agents-core| transport stream shared helpers > falls back to generic message when errorMessage is absent 0ms
 ✓ |agents-core| transport stream shared helpers > finalizes successful transport streams 2ms
 ✓ |agents-core| transport stream shared helpers > marks transport stream failures and runs cleanup 1ms
 ✓ |agents-support| transport stream shared helpers > throws with the original error message when stream failed 1ms
 ✓ |agents-support| transport stream shared helpers > throws with the abort error message when stream was aborted 0ms
 ✓ |agents-support| transport stream shared helpers > falls back to generic message when errorMessage is absent 0ms
 ✓ |agents-support| transport stream shared helpers > finalizes successful transport streams 2ms
 ✓ |agents-support| transport stream shared helpers > marks transport stream failures and runs cleanup 1ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  22 passed (22)
   Duration  2.00s (transform 1.34s, setup 853ms, import 518ms, tests 284ms, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: `finalizeTransportStream` throws `output.errorMessage` when set; falls back to `"An unknown error occurred"` when `output.errorMessage` is absent.

What was not tested: live provider transport failures requiring a running provider environment.

- `2e3ce45fca`

  - Updated `docker-compose.yml` to run `openclaw-gateway` and `openclaw-cli` as `${OPENCLAW_UID:-1000}:${OPENCLAW_GID:-1000}`

This keeps bind-mounted files owned by the host user and avoids workspace permission drift when editing files inside/outside containers.

target.title: # PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)
target.body:
# PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)

## Summary

This PR introduces a stricter, policy-driven filesystem access layer (**PathGuard**) for OpenClaw agent filesystem tools, including local media (image/pdf) loaders.

It closes key security and consistency gaps by:
- canonicalizing requested paths before evaluation (realpath-aware, ENOENT-safe),
- preventing traversal and symlink/junction escapes,
- enforcing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths` consistently for classic filesystem tools **and** local media loads,
- preserving **deny-overrides-allow** semantics,
- anchoring workspace-relative policy entries to the workspace root (and re-anchoring after realpath),
- treating `allowedPaths: []` as an explicit **deny-all**,
- supporting absolute glob policy entries (via minimatch) alongside canonical absolute literals,
- returning structured, remediation-focused policy denial tool results (`policy_denied`) when access is blocked,
- and enforcing a monotonic filesystem policy ceiling for spawned subagents (global + agent + subagent defaults + spawn-time tightening), plumbed end-to-end into the spawned run.

**Important scope note:**

R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”

Remote URLs (`http(s)://`) and inline `data:` inputs are not treated as filesystem paths and are outside this layer.

---

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Tests

---

## Linked Issues

- Closes #39672
- Related #8719

---

## Problem Statement (What was wrong before)

Before this PR, filesystem tool enforcement had multiple gaps:

1) **Path canonicalization and symlink escape handling were fragmented**
- Traversal protections and “what path are we actually touching?” logic were spread across tools/flows.
- Non-existent paths (ENOENT) were tricky: naive realpath calls fail and can allow later-creation escapes.

2) **Policy matching was inconsistent**
- Absolute vs relative entries could be evaluated in different “path spaces”.
- Glob vs non-glob entries could diverge in behavior, especially when “roots” filtering was used as an approximation.

3) **Media tools could diverge from file tool policy**
- Local file references inside image/pdf flows could reopen outside-workspace reads if not enforced per resolved file.

4) **Sandbox and host flows lacked a unified enforcement story**
- Enforcement needed to be identical regardless of whether the run is sandboxed or host-based.

5) **Spawned subagents did not have a guaranteed filesystem policy ceiling**
- Subagents needed an explicit, monotonic ceiling that could not be loosened at spawn time.

---

## What Changed (Design + Implementation)

### 1) Core PathGuard enforcement (`src/security/path-guard.ts`)

PathGuard implements:

- **Canonical resolution for existing paths** via `fs.realpath`.
- **Nearest-existing-parent resolution** for ENOENT paths (prevents “create later via symlink” bypass).
- **Symlink/junction-aware comparisons** in canonical path space.
- **Deny precedence**: deny rules always override allow rules.
- **Workspace-only boundary**: `workspaceOnly` blocks outside-workspace access.
- **Relative entry anchoring**: workspace-relative policy entries are anchored to the workspace root, and re-anchored after realpath resolution.
- **Empty allowlists**: `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit deny-all.
- **Absolute policy entries**:
  - absolute **literals** are canonicalized before comparison,
  - absolute **globs** are supported (matched against canonical real paths) without attempting to realpath the pattern.
- **Glob detection** via `Minimatch(...).hasMagic()` (brace/extglob supported).

### 2) Filesystem tool integration (read/write/edit/apply_patch-style)

- Tool FS config is resolved through the tool/fs policy configuration and enforced using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`.
- Wrappers normalize args and run PathGuard checks on resolved paths.
- `apply_patch` is explicitly guarded by parsing patch hunks and enforcing FS policy on each patch target path (not just args.path/file_path).

### 3) Structured `policy_denied` tool result (model guidance)

When PathGuard denies access, tools return a structured tool result (instead of only throwing a raw error) that:
- states the restriction is system-enforced and cannot be bypassed,
- describes what the tool attempted to do,
- provides actionable remediation options,
- and explicitly points to config keys that can be changed to grant access.

This improves model behavior: fewer useless retries, more direct user remediation.

### 4) Media tool integration (image/pdf local paths)

Local media loads are now policy-checked on the resolved file path:
- `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts`

Key invariant:
- enforcement is per resolved path using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`, not via root-prefix approximations.

### 5) Sandbox behavior

For local media loads in sandbox mode:
- PathGuard runs in sandbox flows too,
- policy root uses `sandbox.root` when sandboxed,
- otherwise `workspaceDir` is used.

### 6) Subagent filesystem policy ceiling (spawn-time tightening + runtime enforcement)

Goal: spawned subagents must never receive broader filesystem access than the configured ceiling.

Policy sources (monotonic tightening):
- Global: `tools.fs`
- Per-agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- Subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- Sessions-spawn defaults/ceiling: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (applied when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`)
- Spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge semantics:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: enforced as a ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

Persistence + plumbing:
- Effective policy is persisted on the spawned child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) via `sessions.patch`.
- Gateway plumbs it into the child run as ingress field `toolFsPolicy`.
- Gateway session resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve `spawnedToolFsPolicy` (no widening after reset).
- Embedded runner passes it into tool construction.
- `createOpenClawCodingTools` accepts an `fsPolicy` override and uses it as the effective policy for that run.
- Nested spawns inherit the parent session’s `spawnedToolFsPolicy` ceiling before applying config/tool-call tightening.

---

## Configuration (for contributors)

### Filesystem policy knobs

- `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: boolean`
- `tools.fs.allowedPaths: string[] | undefined` (empty array means deny-all)
- `tools.fs.denyPaths: string[] | undefined`

Subagent ceiling:
- `tools.subagents.fs.{workspaceOnly, allowedPaths, denyPaths}`

Sessions-spawn defaults + spawn-time tightening:
- `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (config default ceiling/tightening)
- `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (tool arg tightening; can only further restrict)

Notes:
- Deny always wins.
- Relative entries are workspace-anchored.
- Policies are evaluated against canonicalized real paths.

---

## Explicit Non-Goals / Out of Scope

- Exec sandboxing is not implemented here.
  R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”
- No shell argument parsing.
- Remote source governance is out-of-scope.

---

## Implementation Notes (for contributors)

### Policy evaluation edge cases covered

- `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit **deny-all** (not “no restriction”).
- Relative literal entries are **re-anchored after realpath**: if a workspace-relative entry canonicalizes outside the workspace (e.g. it points at a symlink to an external dir), it **does not** match outside-workspace targets.
- Absolute glob entries support symlink-alias authoring by **canonicalizing the non-glob prefix** before matching.
  - When rebuilding the rewritten pattern, the implementation **normalizes slash joining** so root-anchored globs stay semantically equivalent (e.g. `"/**/*.pem"` must not become `"//**/*.pem"`).

### Tool behavior on denial (model guidance)

When a filesystem access is blocked by PathGuard, tools return a structured `policy_denied` result (English text + structured fields) so models:
- do not attempt “bypass retries”,
- clearly communicate what was attempted,
- present actionable remediation options,
- and point to config keys (`tools.fs.*`) for operators to change if access is intended.

### Subagent filesystem ceiling (monotonic tightening)

Effective subagent policy is computed as a ceiling and is monotonic (cannot be widened by spawn calls):
- global: `tools.fs`
- agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- sessions-spawn defaults: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`
- spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` / tool arg `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge intent:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

The effective ceiling is persisted on the child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) and forwarded into the embedded runner (`toolFsPolicy` → `createOpenClawCodingTools({ fsPolicy })`).

---

## Verification

- CI checks and tests pass (per latest runs).
- Added/maintained coverage ensuring `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` is applied as a default tightening when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`.
- Verified critical denial scenarios: traversal, symlink/junction escapes, ENOENT parent-walk, deny precedence, empty allowlist deny-all, sandbox media enforcement, apply_patch enforcement, and subagent ceiling propagation.
- Verified monotonic nested spawning: a tightened subagent cannot spawn a child with broader FS access (parent `spawnedToolFsPolicy` is inherited).
- Verified gateway resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve the spawned FS ceiling (no widening after reset).

---

## Reviewer / Contributor Notes (Do not regress these invariants)

- Do not replace per-path enforcement with root-prefix approximations for glob policies.
- Keep deny-overrides-allow.
- Keep relative policy entries anchored to workspace (including after realpath).
- Keep sandbox and host local-file enforcement aligned.
- Keep local filesystem policy and remote-source policy separate concepts.
- Keep subagent policy tightening monotonic (no privilege escalation vs ceiling).
- Remember: exec is outside this enforcement layer.

---

## Rollback / Recovery

- Disable restrictions by setting `workspaceOnly: false` and clearing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths`.
- No data migration required.

target.title: fix(agents): detect empty provider responses as failures, improve Telegram error routing

## Summary

- Adds `EmptyResponseError` typed class in `model-fallback.ts`; replaces the fragile string-matched `new Error("Empty response content received from provider")`

- In Telegram dispatch: tracks `lastError` via `onError` and an outer try/catch; routes the user-visible fallback message by error type (rate limit / empty response / general); re-throws aborts and timeouts so they don't produce spurious fallback replies; re-throws other dispatcher crashes after cleanup so callers still see the failure for logging/alerting

target.title: memory: add recent continuity snapshots
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: long-running sessions can keep transcripts and memory archives but still lose the clearest current in-flight state across `/new`, `/reset`, and compaction.
- Why it matters: the next turn can feel detached even when history exists, which shows up as partial continuity loss rather than missing storage.
- What changed: add an additive `memory/recent` continuity layer with `latest.md` plus timestamped snapshots, wire it into reset/new, compact-before, and post-compaction recovery, and place the continuity helpers under the current `packages/memory-host-sdk` layout used by upstream main.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no retrieval ranking changes, no rollback journal/promotion, no dream/background scheduler, and no replacement of QMD/vector memory/transcripts.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #60742
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: N/A
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: feature slice, not a regression fix

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `/new` and `/reset` still write archive notes, and now also write a recent continuity snapshot.
- `session:compact:before` writes a recent continuity snapshot without requiring a full archive note.
- Post-compaction recovery can read the recent continuity layer before broader history.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[active work] -> [reset/compact] -> [broad history survives] -> [current task state is less obvious]

After:
[active work] -> [recent continuity snapshot] -> [reset/compact] -> [recent state restored first]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - The runtime now writes an additional recent continuity file in the existing workspace memory area.
  - Mitigation: additive only, same workspace boundary, no new external transport, and no broader retrieval/promotion behavior in this slice.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local development checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for core file-writing/recovery wiring; continuity extraction helper covered in local test flow
- Integration/channel (if any): none
- Relevant config (redacted): default local workspace memory flow

### Steps

1. Trigger `/new` or `/reset` on a session with active work state.
2. Trigger `session:compact:before` and inspect the resulting workspace memory files.
3. Run post-compaction recovery context generation.

### Expected

- `memory/recent/latest.md` and snapshots reflect the current in-flight task state.
- Recovery helpers surface recent continuity before broader history.

### Actual

- Local tests pass for continuity parsing/rendering, archive/reset wiring, compaction safeguard context, and post-compaction refresh.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Focused validation run locally:

- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.test.ts`
- `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/post-compaction-context.test.ts`
- `src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts`
- Result: 4 files, 132 tests passed
- `tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit` passed

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - recent continuity documents render and parse round-trip
  - material-change detection avoids noisy snapshot rewrites
  - reset/new paths continue archive writes and add continuity writes
  - post-compaction helpers and safeguard summaries include recent continuity
- Edge cases checked:
  - timestamp-only changes do not force rewrites
  - compact-before path can write continuity without full archive note
- What you did **not** verify:
  - live rollout against an actively running production workspace

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk:
  - Recent continuity snapshots could add noise if written too often.
  - Mitigation:
    - material-change detection avoids rewriting on trivial timestamp-only changes.
- Risk:
  - Review scope could sprawl into broader memory architecture discussion.
  - Mitigation:
    - this slice is intentionally limited to recent continuity only, with follow-up work kept out of this PR.

target.title: fix: strip <relevant-memories> injected by memory plugin from user messages in WebUI
target.body:
## Summary

- Memory plugin's `before_agent_start` hook prepends `<relevant-memories>...</relevant-memories>` to user prompts before they are stored in session history
- `stripEnvelopeFromMessages()` (server) and `processMessageText` (WebUI) only stripped these tags from **assistant** messages, never from **user** messages
- This caused internal memory context to appear in the user message bubble in WebUI

## Root cause

`stripRelevantMemoriesTags` was only reachable via `stripAssistantInternalScaffolding`, which is only called for `role === "assistant"`. User messages went through `stripInboundMetadata` + `stripEnvelope`, neither of which handles `<relevant-memories>` XML tags.

## Fix

- Export `stripRelevantMemoriesTags` from `assistant-visible-text.ts`
- `chat-sanitize.ts` (`stripEnvelopeFromMessage`): apply `stripRelevantMemoriesTags` to user-role message content — fixes the `chat.history` RPC path
- `message-extract.ts` (`processMessageText`): apply `stripRelevantMemoriesTags` for user-role messages — fixes the WebUI rendering path
- Add regression tests in `chat-sanitize.test.ts` and `message-extract.test.ts` for both string content and array content shapes

## Test plan

- [ ] `npx vitest run src/gateway/chat-sanitize.test.ts` — 11 tests pass
- [ ] `npx vitest run ui/src/ui/chat/message-extract.test.ts` — 6 tests pass
- [ ] Start OpenClaw with `memory-lancedb` extension enabled, send a message, verify user bubble shows only the actual message text

Fixes #59568

target.title: Replace SHA-1 with SHA-256 for config fingerprinting
target.body:
## Summary

- Replace `crypto.createHash("sha1")` with `crypto.createHash("sha256")` in two files:
  - `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts` — MCP server catalog fingerprinting
  - `src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts` — temp media file content hashing in tests

## Motivation

SHA-1 is used here for MCP server catalog fingerprinting and test file caching. The rest of the codebase consistently uses SHA-256 (e.g., `safeEqualSecret` in `src/security/secret-equal.ts`). This change aligns these two remaining SHA-1 call sites with the project's standard hash algorithm.

No functional impact — these hashes are used for cache invalidation and content-addressing, not integrity verification. SHA-256 is a drop-in replacement.

target.title: Add prototype pollution guard to resolveConfigPath
target.body:
## Summary

`resolveConfigPath` in `src/shared/config-eval.ts` traverses config objects by dot-separated path strings (e.g. `"foo.bar.baz"`), but does not check whether any path segment is a dangerous prototype key (`__proto__`, `prototype`, or `constructor`). This means a crafted path string could be used to access or leak prototype chain properties.

Other path-traversal functions in the codebase already guard against this:
- `parseConfigPath` in `src/config/config-paths.ts`
- `merge-patch.ts`, `runtime-overrides.ts`, `includes.ts`, and others

All of these use the shared `isBlockedObjectKey` utility from `src/infra/prototype-keys.ts`.

## Changes

- Import `isBlockedObjectKey` from `../infra/prototype-keys.js`
- Add a guard inside the `resolveConfigPath` loop that returns `undefined` when a blocked key is encountered
- Matches the existing security pattern used across the codebase

## Test plan

- [ ] Existing tests for `resolveConfigPath` and `isConfigPathTruthyWithDefaults` continue to pass
- [ ] Paths containing `__proto__`, `prototype`, or `constructor` segments now return `undefined`
- [ ] Normal dot-separated paths still resolve correctly

target.title: docs(skills): add advanced features section to creating-skills guide
target.body:
## Summary

Add an "Advanced features" section to `docs/tools/creating-skills.md` that bridges the gap between the basic getting-started guide and the full skills reference.

### Problem

As reported in #39681, the creating-skills guide only covers a bare-bones hello-world example. Critical skill authoring features (conditional activation, API key injection, multi-command dispatch, invocation control, template variables) are only discoverable by reading the full `skills.md` reference — or never discovered at all.

### Changes

Added a new "Advanced features" section covering:

- **Conditional activation** — `requires.bins`, `requires.env`, `requires.config`
- **API key and env injection** — `skills.entries.<name>.apiKey` / `.env`
- **Invocation control** — `user-invocable`, `disable-model-invocation`
- **Multi-command skills** — `command-dispatch: tool` with `command-tool`
- **Template variables** — `{baseDir}`
- **Publishing to ClawHub**

Each item links to the relevant section in the skills reference for details.

Closes #39681

target.body: # PR: Enable Text Interaction via ModalBottomSheet (Closes #57754) ## Summary - Problem: Users were unable to copy, share, or quote standard chat message text on the Android app (only code blocks were selectable), causing significant friction for mobile users. - Why it matters: Mobile users frequently need to extract, share, or reply to specific parts of AI responses. Switching to a desktop just to copy text is a broken UX. - What changed: Implemented a native Material 3 `ModalBottomSheet` triggered by a long-press on chat bubbles, offering 4 core actions: Copy, Select Copy (via a dedicated `SelectionContainer` dialog), Share, and Reply. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Core message markdown rendering logic, backend API contracts, and project build files (AGP/Java toolchain changes were intentionally kept out of this PR to avoid CI/CD breaks). --- ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra --- ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra --- ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #57754 - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression --- ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) N/A --- ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A --- ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - **New Interaction:** Long-pressing any chat bubble now slides up a native Bottom Sheet instead of doing nothing. - **New Actions:** Users can now tap: - **Copy** → copies entire text to clipboard - **Select Copy** → opens a scrollable dialog for precise selection - **Share** → opens Android system share sheet - **Reply** → quotes the message in the composer --- ## Diagram ```text Before: [Long Press on Chat Bubble] -> [No Action / Only code blocks selectable] -> [User frustration] After: [Long Press on Chat Bubble] -> [ModalBottomSheet Opens] ├─> [Tap 'Copy'] -> [Text in Clipboard] ├─> [Tap 'Select Copy'] -> [Selection Dialog Opens] -> [Native Text Selection] ├─> [Tap 'Share'] -> [Android Share Sheet Opens] └─> [Tap 'Reply'] -> [Message Quoted in Composer] ``` --- ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A --- ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Android (Physical Device / Emulator) - Runtime/container: Android OS (Jetpack Compose UI) - Model/provider: N/A (UI layer) - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Launch the OpenClaw Android app and open any chat thread. 2. Long-press on a standard text message bubble. 3. Observe the `ModalBottomSheet` sliding up with 4 actions. 4. Tap 'Select Copy' to verify the `SelectionContainer` dialog opens and allows precise text selection. ### Expected - Bottom sheet opens smoothly without interfering with the `LazyColumn` scroll state. - All 4 actions execute their respective device-level intents natively. ### Actual - Bottom sheet opens as expected. - 'Select Copy' dialog scales properly based on content size up to a maximum height. --- ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) **Screenshots:** - - - --- ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** Tested all 4 actions (Copy, Select Copy, Share, Reply) on long and short messages. Verified that the Reply action properly populates the chat composer state. - **Edge cases checked:** - Very long text handled using `.heightIn(max = 400.dp)` - Prevented crashes using `?.let` instead of `!!` - **Not verified:** End-to-end integration with live WebSocket messages (tested with local UI state). --- ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. --- ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` --- ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** Unnecessary recompositions of the `LazyColumn` when bottom sheet state changes. - **Mitigation:** - State is hoisted using `remember` at bubble level - Used `?.let` for safe rendering - Scoped recomposition only to interacted component

target.title: feat(cron): show before/after diff in `cron edit` before applying changes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `cron edit` applied changes silently with no preview, making it easy to accidentally misconfigure a job without realising until the next run.
- Why it matters: Cron schedules and payloads are critical to correct agent operation; silent mutations increase the risk of unintended changes going unnoticed.
- What changed: Added a before/after diff preview to `cron edit`. The diff is sent to stderr so it doesn't corrupt `--json` output. Uses a stable sorted-key JSON serialisation for comparison, deduplicates `cron.list` calls, makes the preflight list fetch non-blocking (failures are warned, not fatal), and correctly handles replace-style fields (`schedule`, `payload`) vs merge-style fields in the preview.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Actual update logic (`cron.update` RPC) is unchanged. The diff is informational only; it does not gate the update.

## Change Type (select all)
- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR
- Closes #59453
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)
- Root cause: N/A – new feature, not a regression.
- Missing detection / guardrail:
- Prior context:
- Why this regressed now:
- If unknown, what was ruled out:

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cli/cron-cli/`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Preview shows correct before/after for schedule and payload fields; sorted-key serialisation eliminates key-order noise.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Unit tests on `computeDisplayAfter*` functions with mock job data.
- Existing test that already covers this: N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — unit tests cover the preview logic.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`cron edit` now prints a before/after diff to stderr before applying changes.

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification (required)
- Verified scenarios: `cron edit <id> --every 2h` shows before/after diff on stderr; actual job is updated correctly. `cron edit <id> --json` diff goes to stderr, JSON result to stdout (no corruption).
- Edge cases checked: Editing `schedule` when `staggerMs` not in patch correctly preserves existing `staggerMs` in preview. Editing `payload` with same `kind` shows field-level merge preview.
- What you did not verify: Preview accuracy when gateway returns unexpected job shape; concurrent edits.

## Review Conversations
- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

---

## Summary

Before `cron edit` applies its patch, it now fetches the current job and prints a human-readable diff showing exactly which fields will change.

## Before / After

```
$ openclaw cron edit <job-id> --name new-name --disable
Applying changes:
  name: old-name → new-name
  enabled: true → false
{...updated job JSON...}
```

## Behavior

- Fetches current job via `cron.list` before patching
- Shows only fields that actually differ (skips unchanged fields)
- Silently skips diff if job not found (e.g. race condition or gateway offline)
- No diff shown when patch is empty
- Existing JSON output (`writeJson`) is unchanged
- `--yes` flag skips the confirmation prompt for scripting use cases

## Testing

- Added unit tests covering: diff displayed for changed fields, unchanged fields skipped, empty patch (no diff), `--yes` flag bypasses confirmation, gateway offline (silent skip)
- Ran `pnpm test src/cli/cron-cli/` locally — all tests pass

## Related

Closes #59453

target.title: feat: add context visualization in tui
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The `/context` command in the TUI only outputs static, text-based context breakdowns with no interactivity or visual representation of context composition.
- Why it matters: Understanding context usage and growth is critical for debugging prompt engineering, identifying oversized bootstrap files, and monitoring token utilization during sessions.
- What changed: Added an interactive TUI overlay invoked via `/context visualize` (or `/context viz`) that renders color-coded bar charts, a proportional stacked bar, a growth sparkline, and drill-down views for each context category (system prompt, workspace files, skills, tools).
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): The existing `/context`, `/context list`, `/context detail`, and `/context json` gateway command behavior is untouched. No new gateway endpoints were added; the overlay reuses the existing `sessions.usage` endpoint with `includeContextWeight: true`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes # N/A
- Related # N/A
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `/context visualize` (or `/context viz`) in the TUI opens an interactive overlay with color-coded bar charts, a growth sparkline, and token utilization percentage.
- Keyboard navigation: `j/k` or arrows to navigate categories, `Enter` to drill into a category's items, `Esc`/`Backspace` to go back, `r` to refresh, `q` to close.
- Live auto-refresh every 2 seconds while the overlay is open.
- `/context` now appears in slash command autocomplete with subcommand completions (`visualize`, `viz`, `list`, `detail`, `json`).
- Bare `/context` and existing subcommands (`list`, `detail`, `json`) continue to forward to the gateway as before.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
/context [any] -> gateway message -> static text reply

After:
/context visualize|viz -> local TUI overlay (interactive, live-updating)
/context [list|detail|json|help|<empty>] -> gateway message -> static text reply (unchanged)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` (reuses existing `sessions.usage` endpoint)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: Any
- Integration/channel (if any): TUI

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm dev` to start the TUI
2. Type `/context viz` and press Enter
3. Use `j/k` to navigate categories, `Enter` to drill into one
4. Press `Esc` to return to overview, `q` or `Esc` to close
5. Verify `/context list` still returns static text from gateway

### Expected

- Interactive overlay renders with bar charts, sparkline, and token utilization
- Drill-down shows per-item breakdowns (files, skills, tools)
- Overlay auto-refreshes every 2s with visible repaint
- Existing `/context list|detail|json` subcommands work unchanged

### Actual

- (To be verified manually)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

All 202 TUI tests pass (`pnpm test -- src/tui/`). Updated 5 test cases in `tui-command-handlers.test.ts` that used `/context` as an "unknown command" example (changed to `/foobar`). TypeScript type-check passes (`pnpm tsgo`) with no new errors.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: TUI test suite (202 tests, 18 files pass), type-check, format/lint
- Edge cases checked: Updated tests that relied on `/context` being an unknown command; verified `/context` without args still forwards to gateway
- What you did **not** verify: Manual TUI overlay rendering (requires running gateway)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The 2-second polling interval could add unnecessary gateway load if many TUI clients leave the overlay open.
  - Mitigation: Polling only runs while the overlay is open and stops on close via `clearInterval`. The `sessions.usage` endpoint is lightweight and already exists.

The current Himalaya skill/reference docs still show commands like global `himalaya --account ...`, `flag add --flag ...`, and the old move/copy argument order. Those examples fail against installed `himalaya v1.1.0`.

- `himalaya message move 42 Archive`

## Scope boundary

- no runtime integration changes
- no provider transport changes
- docs/skill/reference updates only
